steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content: Before the blank: “The book wasn't written solely by” plus [Legler] forms an independent clause. After the blank: “other authors, such as Stephen Graham Jones and Barry Lopez, also contributed essays” is also an independent clause (subject: “other authors”; verb: “contributed”; the phrase “such as...” is an appositive providing examples). The word “however” signals a contrast between these two ideas—the book had limited authorship (Legler), but multiple authors actually contributed.

steps.title: Apply the conjunction rule for conjunctive adverbs
steps.content: When joining two independent clauses with a conjunctive adverb like “however,” the standard pattern is: independent clause + semicolon + conjunctive adverb + comma + independent clause. This requires a semicolon before “however” and a comma after. Legler; however, delivers this pattern correctly. Choice A creates a comma splice (joining two independent clauses with commas and a transition word). Choice C starts a new sentence, which is grammatically acceptable but the SAT prefers the semicolon form when both options are available. Choice D reverses the punctuation (comma before, semicolon after), which violates the rule. That matches choice B — B is correct.
